one_line_takeaway: By forcing affirmative-token logits up and denial-token logits down at inference on white-box open-source LLMs, SSAG/VulMine recovers safety-suppressed harmful content with 95.65% average ASR and ~86% less time than prior white-box attacks, exposing logit suppression as a systemic weakness of current alignment.
key_finding: VulMine achieves an average ASR of 95.65% on the AdvBench subset across five models, beating the best baseline (LAA, 80.91% avg) by 14.74 points and winning on all five models, with Wilcoxon p-values far below 0.05 against every baseline. It is especially strong on well-defended Llama-2 models (95.93% on Llama-2-7b-chat, 96.16% on Llama-2-13b-chat) where template/black-box attacks (PAIR, GPTFuzzer) stay under 60%. The empirical study shows harmful-content rate rises with affirmative-prefix length (avg 6.38% question-only -> 25.00% with 'Sure!' -> 31.37% with a complete prefix), and that >99% of denial responses fall into four patterns (Apology, Identification, Incapacity, Notation), with Incapacity (54.24%) and Identification (33.48%) dominating. VulMine also reduces exposure time by ~86% on average versus token-level methods and stays competitive against defenses (avg ASR 77.39% vs ESF, 73.05% vs SmoothLLM, 67.23% vs PAT; overall ~72.56%), indicating existing defenses mostly detect jailbreak prompts rather than fixing the underlying logit-suppression weakness.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SSAG (Semantic-sensitive Alignment and Generation), a white-box jailbreak technique that exploits the observation that LLM safety alignment works by suppressing (not eliminating) the logits of affirmative/harmful tokens. SSAG recovers this suppressed harmful content by directly manipulating output-layer logits at inference time, without altering any model weights. The authors implement SSAG as an automated tool, VulMine, which runs in three phases: (1) few-shot templating with a rephrase LLM to auto-generate an affirmative target response (e.g. 'Sure! Here is a tutorial on making a bomb'); (2) prompt-agnostic logit manipulation that forces k affirmative-response logits to +infinity, drives denial-token logits to -infinity, and boosts a sampled top-K token, using a trained sorting model to rank candidate prefixes; (3) logit-guided autoregressive generation of the full harmful response. The contribution is the attack method and tool, plus an empirical study of how affirmative-prefix length and denial-pattern structure relate to harmful-content elicitation. Note: the paper uses SSAG (the method) and VulMine (the tool) largely interchangeably in its results.
threat_model: White-box attacker with full access to an open-source model's parameters, architecture, and output logits (the paper states the attack 'requires white-box access'). The motivating exploitation scenario is a supply-chain / model-provenance attack: an attacker downloads a public open-source LLM, embeds a lightweight logit-modifying patch that elevates suppressed harmful responses on trigger prompts while preserving benign behavior and leaving the model weights unchanged (making it hard to trace), then repackages and republishes the model as an 'improved/fine-tuned' variant. Downstream developers who integrate or further fine-tune it silently inherit the covert harmful behavior. The attacker goal is reliable elicitation of policy-violating content while evading prompt-level jailbreak defenses and perplexity filters.
limitations: Requires white-box access to logits, so it does not apply to closed/API-only commercial models (all five targets are open-source). Evaluated only on five 7B-13B open-source models; no larger or newer frontier models. The attack fundamentally relies on the assumption that alignment merely suppresses rather than removes harmful logits. Effectiveness is uneven and defense-dependent: against PAT on Mistral-7b-Instruct ASR drops to 57.69%, and perplexity is slightly higher than prompt-engineering attacks (though same order of magnitude). ASR is scored by an automated JUDGE (Llama-Guard-3) rather than human verification, so measured harmfulness inherits the judge's error profile. The abstract/Table 4 caption contain naming inconsistencies ('VulMine' listed as a dataset; SSAG vs VulMine used interchangeably), and the headline abstract figures (95% success, 77% ASR) conflate the AdvBench ASR with the against-defenses ASR. No new defense is proposed; the paper only calls for more robust alignment.

datasets_used: AdvBench (520-behavior subset from Zou et al. [30]; primary evaluation benchmark)
JailbreakBench (100 harmful behaviors across 10 categories; used in the empirical study of prefix length)
quantitative_results: VulMine average ASR on AdvBench across 5 models: 95.65% (best baseline LAA: 80.91%; +14.74 points)
Per-model VulMine ASR: Llama-2-7b-chat 95.93%, Llama-2-13b-chat 96.16%, Mistral-7b-Instruct 98.07%, Llama-3-8b-Instruct 98.07%, gemma-7b-it 90.00%
Average exposure/response time reduction ~86% vs token-level methods
ASR against defenses: ESF 77.39% avg, SmoothLLM 73.05% avg, PAT 67.23% avg (overall ~72.56%)
Empirical harmful-content rate: question-only 6.38% avg -> +'Sure!' 25.00% avg -> +complete prefix 31.37% avg
Denial-pattern distribution (avg): Incapacity 54.24%, Identification 33.48%, Apology 9.48%, Notation 1.99%, Others 0.81% (>99% coverage)
Ablation ASR (avg): full VulMine 95.65%, VulMine-k 68.52%, VulMine-m 52.48%, question-only 4.92% (k contributes 16.04 points more than m)
Sorting model Gamma: F1 = 0.9207 at m=5 (two-layer FC net, hidden 512, gtr-t5-xl 768-dim embeddings, 300 train / 700 test, 1000 epochs, lr 1e-3)
Settings: prefix length m=5, batch size N=2000, temperature=1, single NVIDIA A100 GPU
Wilcoxon signed-rank p-values vs baselines all << 0.05 (e.g., 2.6e-55 vs PAIR, 2.2e-39 vs GCG)
baselines_compared: GCG (white-box, gradient-based adversarial suffix; avg ASR 68.79%)
LAA (white-box prompt engineering + gradient search; avg ASR 80.91%)
COLD / COLD-Attack (white-box logit-based controllable decoding; avg ASR 63.08%)
